content.full_text:
California
Fair P01itical
Practices Cornrnission
August 14, 1991
Paul, o. cornier
SAFE BIDCO
145 wikiup Drive
santa Rosa, CA 95403
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I -91- 14 0
Dear Mr. cornier:
You have requested advice concerning the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (trActr).r/ 
Your request is
one for general assistance. Accordingly, Ide are treating it as a
request for informal assistance. z /
OUESTIONS
1. 
Is it possible for the State Assistance Fund for
Enterprise Business and Industrial Developnent Corporation (ttSAFE
BIDCo'r) to drop or rescind its conflict of interest code because
the designated employees are also covered by the State Snall
Business Development Program's (rrslna1l business progran") conflict
of interest code? If not, is it acceptable to tnake SAFE BIDcO,s
code identical to that of the snall business prograrn?
2. 
l,tust consultants file assuming, annual and leaving
office statements, or is there a one-time disclosure for
consu I tants ?
L/ Governnent Code sections 81ooo-9101.5. AlL statutory
references are to the Governnent code unless otherwise indicated.
Connission regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations
Section 180oo, et seq. AII references to regulations are to Title
2. Division 5 of the california code of Regulations.
2/ 
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal uritten advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 cal. Code of Regs. Section
1832e (c) (3) . )
428 J Strcct, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 . Sacramcnto CA 95tlO4-trSO7 . (916) JZ2-566O


Our File No. I-91-140
Page 2
3. 
One of SAFE BIDCo,s directors is an attorney/smalI
business consultant. Must he disclose fees that he receives from
his small business clients?
Likewise, must a director who is a business broker and real
estate salesman disclose commission income on each sale that he
rnakes involving snaII business owners?
coNcLUs Ioivs
1. 
SAFE BIDCO, as a state agency, is required to maintain a
conflict of interest code, seperate and distinct from the conflict
of interest code of the snall business program.
Agencies should not simply duplicate a conflict of interest
code fron another agency even if it is thought that the two
agencies are similarily structured. Each agency,s conflict of
interest code is designed specifically to neet the needs of the
agency, and may not be applicable to other agencies.
2. 
Consultants who must disclose their interests pursuant
to SAFE BIDCO's conflict of interest code are subject to the
filing 
requirements of Government Code Section A7302. All
consultants who are filing statements of economic interests must
file assuming, annuaL and Ieaving office statenents as applicable.
3. 
Fees and commission income are reportabLe if smaLl
business clients/owners are of the tvpe eligible to receive or
which have received in the past two years financial or technical
assistance from SAFE BIDCO.
FACTS
SAFE BIDco's conflict of interest code has been in effect
since 1983. In addition to SAFE BfDCO,s code, several members are
under contract with the state as smalI business developnent board
menbers and are subject to the State SmaII Business Development
Program's conflict of interest code. During our discussion at the
state agency conflict of interest code vrorkshop in san Francisco,
you stated that the disclosure requirements as specified in both
agency's codes are somewhat different from each other making the
filing of the designated employee,s reportable financial inierests
difficult 
and time consuming.
ANALYS IS
The PoLitical Reforn Act was enacted by the people of the
State of California by initiative 
in 1974. The purpose for the
discLosure and disqual i f icat ion provisi.ons of the Act vras to
ensure that public officials, 
whether elected or appointed, would
perform their duties in an irnpartial manner, free from bias caused
by their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons $rho have supported them. (Section 41001(b) .)


Our FiIe No. I-91-140
Page l
Thus, under the Act, every public official3/ 
must disclose
his or her economic interests that could foreseeably be affected
by the exercise of the official's 
duties. 
(Sections 81002(c).
8?200-87313.) Further, Section 871O0 of the Act prohibits any
public official 
from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official 
position to influence a governnental
decision in which the official 
has a financial interest.
The Act also requires that every state and local government
agency adopt a conflict of interest code (Sections 87300 and
87302). A conflict of interest code is a document which lists the
positions r./j.thin the agency that are in decision rnaking capacities
and further provides disclosure categories which describe the
types of information to be reported by the employees $rhose
positions are designated in the code.
An investment, business position, interest in real
property, or source of incorne shall be nade
reportable by the conflict of interest code if the
business entity in which. the investnent or business
position is held, the interest in real property, or
the income or source of incone may foreseeably be
affected naterially by any decision made or
participated in by the designated enployee by
virtue of his or her position.
Section 87302 (a) .
AIl nenbers of SAFE BIDCO's board of directors have been
assigned disclosure category *I which requires the following
reporting respons ib il ities :
Investments, income and business positions in any
business entity which is eligible to receive or
which has received in the past 2 years financial or
technj.cal assistance from the Corporation.
Section 87103 specifies that an official 
has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision vrill have a material financial. effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public AeneraLly, on the official 
or a
menber of his or her imrnediate family or on any economic interest
including:
Any source of incone, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial Iending
Jl 
A ',public official" 
is defined in Section g2048 and
Regulation 18700 as every natural person who is a rnember, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or Local government agency.
Members of the board of directors for SAFE BIDCO are "public
officials" 
as defined i.n the Act. 
(Emphasis added. )


Our File No. 
■-91-■40
Page 4
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the pub).ic without regard to
official 
status. aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars (5250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 :nonths prior to the tine when the
decision is rnade.
section 87103 (c)
However, Section 82030 (a) provides:
rrlncornerr neans, except as provided in subdivision
(b), a payment received, including but not limited
to any salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest,
rent, proceeds from any sale, gift, 
including any
gift of food or beverage, Ioan, forgiveness or
pa)rment of indebtedness received by the filer,
reinbursement for expenses, per diem, or
contribution to an insurance or pension program
paid by any person other than an enployer, and
including any cornrnunity property interest in the
incone of a spouse. Incone aLso includes an
outstanding loan. Income of an indivj.dual. also
includes a pro rata share of any incone of any
business entity or trust in which the individual or
spouse owns, directly. indirectly or beneficially,
a 10-percent interest or greater. 
',Income,,r other
than a gift, 
does not include incorne received from
any source outside the jurisdiction 
and not doing
business within the jurisdiction, 
not planning to
do business within the jurisdiction, 
or not having
done business within the jurisdiction during the
tlro years prior to the time any statenent or other
action is reguired under this tit1e.
Thus, the directors must disclose income they receive from
any business entity covered by their disclosure category. For
real estate brokers, Regulation 18704.3 provides as follows:
The sources of comrnission income in a specific sal.e or
similar transaction include for each of the following:
(2) A real estate broker:
(A) The person the broker represents in the
transaction;
(B) If the broker receives a commission fron
a transaction conducted by an agent working under
the broker,s auspices, the person represented by
the agent;


Our File No. I-91-140
Page 5
(C) Any brokerage business entity through
which the broker conducts business; and
(D) Any person who receives a finder,s or
other referral fee for referring a party to the
transaction to the broker, or who nakes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker.
(3) A real estate agent:
(A) The broker and brokerage business entity
under whose auspices the agent works;
(B) The person the agent represents in the
transaction; and
(C) Any person who receives a finder,s or
other referral fee for referring a party to the
transaction to the broker, or lrho makes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker.
Regulation 18704.3(c) (2) & (c) (3)
Hosrever, if the f il,er,s busj.ness entity is disclosed the
nahes of individual clients of the business do not have to be
disclosed unless the filer,s 
pro rata share of gross recej.pts from
such clients is $J,0,000 or more during the calendar year.
(Section 872o7.1
I hope these general guidelines have addressed your concerns.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please
feel free to contact ne at (916) 3ZZ-59OL.
S incerely,
Scott HalLabrin
Acting ceneral Counsel
